title: Open ('O')
text: Open ('O') Open or create a table, generate Data Definition Language (DDL) to create a table, or create SQL scripts to handle referential constraints. Description Table 1. Open request modes Mode Action Mode 0 Check whether the specified table already exists. If it does exist, open the table. Update the number of open files and open cursors. If the table does not exist, return -1 to Uniface. If the table exists, but cannot be opened, return -4 to Uniface. If the table was successfully opened, return 0 to Uniface. Mode 1 Check whether the specified table already exists. If it does exist, open the table. If it does not exist, create and open the specified table and associated indexes. In both cases, update the number of open files and open cursors. Mode 2 Generate the statements necessary to create the table and indexes. Do not submit the statements to the DBMS, but place them in the SQL buffer. If the length of the statements exceeds the size of the SQL buffer, the service function uaddsql can be used to flush the buffer. The statements must be spread over lines no longer than 250 characters and terminated by a carriage return character. The entire string stored in the SQL buffer must be null-terminated. If the DBMS does not support a DDL, mode 2 of the Open request cannot be implemented. In that case, return -4 to Uniface. This makes it impossible to use the Create Table facility in Uniface. Mode 3 Reserved for internal use. Mode 4 Generate SQL to create referential integrity constraints and associated foreign key rules. If the DBMS does not support declarative referential integrity constraints or triggers, this mode cannot be implemented. In that case, the connector must return -4 to Uniface. This makes it impossible to use the Create Script utility, and it makes it impossible to disable the foreign key rule implementation of Uniface. Mode 5 Generate SQL to drop referential integrity constraints and associated foreign key rules. If the DBMS does not support declarative referential integrity constraints or triggers, this mode cannot be implemented. In that case, the connector must return -4 to Uniface. Mode 6 Generate SQL to check the database for rows which violate referential integrity. One SQL query must be generated for every relationship which is not automatically checked by the DBMS on creation. Typically, connectors generate the query for every relationship which is implemented by means of triggers, not for relationships which are implemented by means of a declarative rule. The query must select the following columns from the rows in the many table which have no matching row in the referenced one table: The primary key columns (to identify the row) The columns in the foreign key of the relationship (to show the nonvalid foreign key) When the (compound) primary key and the (compound) foreign key overlap, the duplicate columns are selected twice. Mode 4, 5 and 6 The connector receives a description of all relationships which apply to the table described by the current control block. The connector encounters every relationship twice; once with the table which has the one role in the relationship and once with the table which has the many role in the relationship. It is up to the connector to ensure that every relationship is implemented only once. The connector can use a simple approach, such as generating every relationship when encountered with the one table. It is also possible for the connector to maintain a dynamic administration of relationships to detect when a particular relationship has already been generated. For this purpose, the connector is informed of the start and end of one logical sequence of Open mode 4, 5 or 6 requests (in which every relationship is encountered exactly two times). This information is passed via uctrl->usubmode . For more information, see the "uctrl" topic. A logical sequence of Open mode 4, 5 or 6 requests consists of only mode 4 requests, only mode 5 requests, or only mode 6 requests. Requests with different modes are not mixed. When the connector returns an error in Open mode 4, 5 or 6, the logical sequence of connector requests is aborted. The first Open mode 4, 5 or 6 thereafter will be the start of a new sequence. The relationships and associated foreign key rules are described in the RelationshipType data structure. For more information, see the "Relationship Type" topic. The generated SQL must not be submitted to the DBMS, but stored in the SQL buffer. The service function uaddsql can be used to flush the buffer. From Uniface Preconditions realized by Uniface—When the Open request is called with mode 0 or mode 1, the connector is logged on to the DBMS and the specified table is not open. In the Open mode 2, 4, 5 and 6 requests, there is no guarantee that the connector is logged on to the DBMS, and the table described by the current control block is not guaranteed to be open or closed. Table 2. Information Available from Uniface Data Structure->Element Description uctrl->TMEnabled Indicates whether the connector is TM-controlled. uctrl->uname Points to the table name. This name must be used to open or create the table. uctrl->urecsiz Record size for the table. uctrl->uflist Points to the field list which describes the fields. uctrl->ufnum Number of elements in the field list. uctrl->uilist Points to the index list. uctrl->uinum Total number of entries in the index list. uctrl->ufstring Points to a string containing the names of the entity and its fields. Offsets in this string are available in the ufname field of the ufldlst structure. uctrl->udrv Points to the connector workspace if a workspace was requested in the Info call. uctrl->udrvini Identification of the current logon path. To Uniface Table 3. Connector Request Return Values Value Description 0 Open was successful. The specified table and indexes are opened. -1 The table does not exist. -4 Exceptional error. -9 I/O server logon error. -16 DBMS reported network error. When the connector request return value is less than 0, an error code must be supplied in uctrl->udrverr . Service Functions udfilcnt — Update the number of open files. udcurscnt — Update the number of open cursors. Extra information Some DBMSs return a file pointer or file handle that is needed to access and close the table. Store this pointer in the connector workspace. For performance reasons, the connector may open multiple channels or cursors to the table (for example, one for reading and one for updating). Refer to the DBMS documentation to see whether this applies to your DBMS. The connector is fully responsible for consistent use of multiple channels. In particular, the connector must close multiple channels with the Close request, and the number of open files and open cursors must be updated correctly by means of udfilcnt and udcurscnt calls. If the DBMS supports different modes for opening tables, first try to open the table in a mode allowing all types of access (sometimes called read/write or shared update access). If opening with this mode fails because of a lack of access rights, open the table in a mode allowing only read access. (This can cause locking problems with some DBMSs, so it is not possible to open a file or table for read-only access unless you also have write privileges.) When one of the connector requests Write, Update, Delete, Wildcard delete/nullify is requested on a table which was opened for read-only access, the connector must return an appropriate connector error to Uniface. If the table to be opened already exists, i n Open mode 0 or mode 1 you can implement a check to compare the description of the existing table with the description of the requested table. If a difference is detected, return -4 to Uniface. This check improves the robustness of database access, but is usually redundant in production environments. Uniface uses the Open mode 2 connector request to implement the Create Table facility and modes 4, 5 and 6 for the Create Script utility, which both produce DDL scripts. Since Create Table scripts are frequently manipulated with a native text editor, make sure that you format the output of Open mode 2 in a manner which is helpful for future use. For Uniface the following formatting conventions apply: The statements must be spread over lines no longer than 250 characters. The statements must be terminated by a carriage return character. The entire string in the SQL buffer must be null-terminated. TM-Controlled Connectors TM-controlled connectors are not allowed to begin, commit, or rollback transactions. Related Links TM-Controlled DBMS Connectors A database connector can be controlled by a transaction manager only if it is TM-capable. Uniface does not provide TM-controlled connectors and does not directly support third-party transaction managers. However, you can use the APIs provided by the Database Connector Interface to build a custom database connector that does. uctrl Exchanges general information between Uniface and the connector, and describes a table. RelationshipType Describes the relationship between two tables. uaddsql Flushes the SQL buffer or the I/O buffer, depending on the mode of the connector request. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
